feedback.text: Hi, one of biggest issues, other than data protection issues and actual enforcement, is the fact that a lot of digital content is locked down behind heavy licensing agreements, despite being mostly unnecessary. Most of those provisions exists to prevent competition between older versions of the same software - you cannot go back to previous version, your license can be terminated at any time with no compensation. This practice creates extremely unhealthy market both for newcomers(existence of walled gardens and interoperability problems) and for customers due to rent seeking behavior from industry giants. For healthy competition user should be always be allowed to downgrade software versions, and feature updates should be separated from security updates if possible. I would like to add that this behavior isn't limited to just software - it encompasses all forms of digital content including books, movies, music etc - things that are marketed at goods, not services. With current legal gray zone, companies can sell you a product marketed as a good, treat it as service, and revoke access at any point in time. Usually with intent to sell you newer version of it - despite your needs being fulfilled by existing one. This has already happened multiple times - Amazon removing books from kindle account(ironically it was Orwell's Animal Farm and 1984 that got removed), Ubisoft removing game(The Crew) they already sold and many other cases. Customers are left without any protections right now. Ideal solution would be to separate Content Creators(software developers, movie publishing etc.) from Content Delivery(online services from which you actually download content) and setup a framework to migrate your digital licenses to any Content Delivery provider you chose as customer. This removes twisted incentives currently present in the system, and it might open a second hand market akin to physical goods. Why one can sell a book(which is usually cheaper) but not an ebook, which is locked down with heavy DRM and licensing agreements - despite fulfilling the same role to end user?
